title: 14 Tahsas — St. Gebre Mesqel & The Assembly
narrative:
title": "14 Tahsas — St. Gebre Mesqel & The Assembly

narrative": "title\": \"14 Tahsas — St. Gebre Mesqel & The Assembly

narrative\": \"The fourteenth day of Tahsas honors Saint Gebre Mesqel, the beloved sixth-century king of Ethiopia whose name in the Ge'ez tongue means 'servant of the Cross,' a title that encapsulates the entire program of his reign. Gebre Mesqel was the son of the great King Kaleb, who had evangelized portions of Arabia, and he inherited both his father's faith and his father's zeal for the establishment of Christianity throughout the Ethiopian highlands. He is credited in the hagiographic tradition with the construction of numerous churches throughout his realm, including the dedication of the great church of Debre Damo, and with the patronage of the Nine Saints whose monastic mission had transformed the spiritual landscape of Ethiopia. His renunciation of his crown in favor of the monastic life in his later years is celebrated as the supreme act of royal kenosis — an earthly king laying aside his glory to follow the King of Heaven who had first laid aside His.

The commemoration of the Holy Cross on this day links the servant of the Cross with the object of his devotion. For the Ethiopian theological tradition, the Cross is never a symbol of defeat but of royal triumph: it is the throne of the victorious King, the tree of life replanted in the midst of the new paradise, the instrument by which the curse of Adam was reversed. The Cross stands at the theological center of the Incarnation itself, for the Word took flesh precisely in order to offer that flesh upon the wood of the cross as the ransom for all flesh; and the month of Tahsas, which moves toward the birth of the Christ Child, moves simultaneously toward the destination of that birth, which is the cross and the resurrection.

The holy martyrs Thyrsus, Leucius, and Callinicus, three witnesses of the Decian persecution in the region of Bithynia, complete this day's assembly. Thyrsus was a layman of good standing who confessed Christ publicly; Leucius was a companion who encouraged him in his confession; Callinicus was a pagan priest who, witnessing the miracles wrought through Thyrsus's prayers during the course of his torments, was converted on the spot and immediately joined him in martyrdom. This triple witness — confessor, companion, and converted pagan priest — speaks of the extraordinary power of the Christian witness to draw the most unexpected souls into the company of the saints.

The theological seal of this fourteenth day of Tahsas is the mystery of royal self-offering. Gebre Mesqel lays aside his crown to serve the Cross; Thyrsus lays aside his security to confess the Crucified; Callinicus lays aside a lifetime of false priesthood to enter the true priesthood of martyrdom. All three reflect the kenosis of the incarnate Word, who though He was rich became poor, who though He was in the form of God did not count equality with God a thing to be grasped but emptied Himself, taking the form of a servant — and it is this servant-king whose birth in Bethlehem this month prepares us to receive.
